channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: CrowdStrike Root Cause Doc: Breakdown of Lessons Learned
executive_summary: CrowdStrike's error correction document reveals significant flaws in their software deployment process, including a lack of testing on production systems and no rollback options for users. The company also failed to implement canary testing and phased rollouts, which are critical for minimizing deployment risks. These oversights highlight the need for higher standards in software deployment and better communication with clients.
bullets: CrowdStrike admitted to having 'glaring issues' in their software deployment process, which should never have existed. This indicates a serious oversight in their quality assurance practices. It's like a chef serving a dish without tasting it first — you can't expect it to be good if you don't check it.

CrowdStrike did not test on their own production deployments, which is a fundamental mistake in software development. This is akin to a pilot flying a plane without ever having practiced in a simulator — the risks are too high. Proper testing ensures that the software behaves as expected in real-world conditions.

There is a need for a structured way to evaluate software deployments on Windows machines, as highlighted in the video. Without a clear evaluation process, it's like navigating a ship without a map — you may end up lost or in dangerous waters. Structured evaluations help identify potential issues before they affect users.

Deployments should include an option to roll back in case of bugs or issues, which CrowdStrike failed to provide. Not having this option is like driving a car without brakes — if something goes wrong, you can't stop it. Rollback options allow for quick recovery from unforeseen problems.

Canary testing and phased rollout are best practices that can minimize the impact of software deployments, yet CrowdStrike did not implement these strategies. Imagine testing a new recipe on a small group of friends before serving it to a large party — it helps catch mistakes early. These practices help ensure that any issues are identified before widespread deployment.

Clients should have more control over the deployment process and be able to assess the risk to their systems, which was not the case with CrowdStrike. This is like giving a passenger no say in the flight path of a plane they are on — they deserve to know and influence their safety. Empowering clients fosters trust and collaboration.

Release notes are essential for transparency and communication with clients, as emphasized in the video. Without them, it's like a book without a table of contents — readers won't know what to expect. Clear release notes help clients understand what changes have been made and how they might affect their systems.

CrowdStrike's apology for software failures was deemed inadequate, involving $10 Uber Eats gift cards that some recipients found fraudulent. This is like giving a band-aid for a broken leg — it doesn’t address the real issue. Thoughtful apologies should acknowledge the impact on clients and offer meaningful solutions.

Cultural issues within a company can contribute to software deployment failures, as noted in the discussion. This is like a sports team with poor communication — without teamwork, they can't win games. Addressing cultural problems is crucial for improving overall performance and accountability.

CrowdStrike's deployment process lacked canary testing and phased rollout, which are critical for reducing risks. Not using these methods is like a firefighter rushing into a burning building without assessing the situation first. Proper testing strategies can save time, resources, and reputation.

The absence of a rollback option in CrowdStrike's deployment process means that clients are left vulnerable to bugs and issues. It's like a tightrope walker without a safety net — one misstep can lead to disaster. Providing a rollback option ensures that clients can recover quickly from unexpected problems.

CrowdStrike's failure to test on their production deployments raises questions about their commitment to quality assurance. This is like a doctor performing surgery without proper training — the consequences can be severe. Rigorous testing is essential to ensure software reliability and client trust.
key_takeaways: Establish a higher standard for software deployment processes to prevent glaring issues. This matters because it ensures reliability and builds client trust. If you don't set these standards, you risk damaging your reputation and losing clients.

Implement structured evaluations for software deployments on Windows machines. This is crucial for identifying potential problems before they affect users. Skipping this step can lead to widespread issues that could have been avoided.

Ensure that all software deployments include a rollback option for clients. This allows for quick recovery from bugs or issues, protecting client systems. Without this option, clients face significant risks if something goes wrong.

Adopt canary testing and phased rollout strategies for software deployments. These practices minimize risks by allowing for early detection of issues. Failing to implement them can lead to larger problems down the line.

Provide clear and comprehensive release notes with every software deployment. This transparency helps clients understand changes and assess their impact. Without release notes, clients may feel left in the dark and lose trust in your company.

technical_terms.Canary Testing: A practice where a new software version is released to a small group of users before a full rollout. Think of it like testing a new flavor of ice cream with a few customers before serving it to everyone.
technical_terms.Phased Rollout: A strategy where software is gradually deployed to users in stages rather than all at once. Like planting seeds in a garden one section at a time to see how they grow before planting the entire field.
technical_terms.Rollback Option: A feature that allows users to revert to a previous version of software if the new version causes problems. Think of it like having a safety net when walking a tightrope — if you slip, you can quickly get back to safety.
technical_terms.Release Notes: Documentation that accompanies a software update, detailing what changes have been made. Like a movie trailer that gives you a sneak peek of what to expect before watching the full film.
technical_terms.Quality Assurance: The process of ensuring that software meets certain standards of quality before it is released. Think of it like a chef tasting a dish to ensure it’s perfect before serving it to guests.
technical_terms.Cultural Issues: Problems within a company’s culture that can affect performance and outcomes. Like a sports team with poor communication — without teamwork, they can't win games.
technical_terms.Structured Evaluation: A systematic approach to assessing software deployments to identify potential issues. Think of it like a thorough health check-up to catch problems before they become serious.
technical_terms.Production Deployments: The final version of software that is used by clients in real-world environments. Like the finished product in a store that customers buy after all the testing is done.
technical_terms.Client Control: The ability for clients to influence and assess the deployment process of software. Like a passenger having a say in the flight path of a plane they are on — they deserve to know and influence their safety.
technical_terms.Testing on Production: The practice of evaluating software in the same environment where it will be used. Think of it like a dress rehearsal for a play, ensuring everything works perfectly before the actual performance.
